([/ˈiːli/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); February 9, 1947 – December 15, 2025) was an American singer, songwriter and guitarist. He was "one of the main movers" of [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas")'s [progressive country](/wiki/Progressive_country "Progressive country") scene in the 1970s and 1980s.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3)

Musical artist

**Joe Ely** ([/ˈiːli/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); February 9, 1947 – December 15, 2025) was an American singer, songwriter and guitarist. He was "one of the main movers" of [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas")'s [progressive country](/wiki/Progressive_country "Progressive country") scene in the 1970s and 1980s.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3)

Ely had a genre-crossing career, performing with [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), [Uncle Tupelo](/wiki/Uncle_Tupelo "Uncle Tupelo"), [Los Super Seven](/wiki/Los_Super_Seven "Los Super Seven"), [The Chieftains](/wiki/The_Chieftains "The Chieftains"), [James McMurtry](/wiki/James_McMurtry "James McMurtry"), [The Clash](/wiki/The_Clash "The Clash"), [Lyle Lovett](/wiki/Lyle_Lovett "Lyle Lovett"), [John Hiatt](/wiki/John_Hiatt "John Hiatt"), and [Guy Clark](/wiki/Guy_Clark "Guy Clark").

## Life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Ely&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and career")]

### Early life and band career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Ely&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life and band career")]

Born Earle Rewell Ely in [Amarillo, Texas](/wiki/Amarillo,_Texas "Amarillo, Texas"), in 1947,[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) Ely spent his teenage years in [Lubbock, Texas](/wiki/Lubbock,_Texas "Lubbock, Texas"),[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) and attended [Monterey High School](/wiki/Monterey_High_School_(Lubbock,_Texas) "Monterey High School (Lubbock, Texas)"). His father Earl worked for the railways, and in Lubbock ran a used clothing store. He died when Ely was aged 13 and his wife was institutionalised. Ely went to live with relatives and contributed to finances by working as a dishwasher and cook in a cafe.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He played [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") from the age of eight and sang in the First Baptist Church choir. He sold his violin to buy an [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") and was expelled from Monterey High School for "singing 'Cherry Pie' by [Marvin & Johnny](/wiki/Marvin_%26_Johnny "Marvin & Johnny") in the middle of a school assembly".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Ely "took to the road like his heroes [Jack Kerouac](/wiki/Jack_Kerouac "Jack Kerouac") and [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie")." He experienced "a drugs bust in Texas involving [magic mushrooms](/wiki/Magic_mushrooms "Magic mushrooms")", went to California where he bought a guitar, and in New York worked as a [janitor](/wiki/Janitor "Janitor") in a theatre.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

He returned to Lubbock and in 1971, with fellow Lubbock musicians [Jimmie Dale Gilmore](/wiki/Jimmie_Dale_Gilmore "Jimmie Dale Gilmore") and [Butch Hancock](/wiki/Butch_Hancock "Butch Hancock"), formed [The Flatlanders](/wiki/The_Flatlanders "The Flatlanders").[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) He recalled, "We never had any money, but we never needed any." There was always "just enough for a bag of rice or a couple of potatoes".[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to Ely, "Jimmie [Gilmore] was like a well of country music. He knew everything about it. And Butch was from the folk world. I was kinda the rock and roll guy and we almost had a triad. We hit it off and started playing a lot together. That opened up a whole new world I had never known existed."

In 1972, the band recorded their first album.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) The band's initial breakup occurred just after their first album was cut and the three musicians followed individual paths, but have appeared together on each other's albums. They reformed in 2002 for *Now Again*.

### Solo career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Ely&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Solo career")]

Ely's first, [self-titled album](/wiki/Joe_Ely_(album) "Joe Ely (album)"), was released in 1977.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) In 1978, his band played London, where he met British [punk rock](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") group [the Clash](/wiki/The_Clash "The Clash").[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Impressed with each other's performances, the two bands later toured together, including appearances in Ely's hometown of Lubbock, as well as [Laredo](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas") and [Ciudad Juárez](/wiki/Ciudad_Ju%C3%A1rez "Ciudad Juárez") in Mexico, across the border from [El Paso, Texas](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas").[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) The Clash paid tribute to Joe Ely by including the lyrics "Well, there ain't no better blend than Joe Ely and his Texas Men" in the lyrics of their song "If Music Could Talk", which was released in 1980 on the album *[Sandinista!](/wiki/Sandinista! "Sandinista!")*[[9]](#cite_note-9) Ely sang backing vocals on the Clash single "[Should I Stay or Should I Go](/wiki/Should_I_Stay_or_Should_I_Go%3F "Should I Stay or Should I Go?")". [Joe Strummer](/wiki/Joe_Strummer "Joe Strummer") planned to record with Ely's band but died before that happened, which was one of Ely's greatest regrets.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On May 1, 1982, Ely presented the Third Annual Tornado Jam in Lubbock to a crowd of 25,000. The Jam included [Linda Ronstadt](/wiki/Linda_Ronstadt "Linda Ronstadt"), [Leon Russell](/wiki/Leon_Russell "Leon Russell"), [Joan Jett](/wiki/Joan_Jett "Joan Jett"), [the Fabulous Thunderbirds](/wiki/The_Fabulous_Thunderbirds "The Fabulous Thunderbirds"), [Jay Boy Adams](/w/index.php?title=Jay_Boy_Adams&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jay Boy Adams (page does not exist)") and [the Crickets](/wiki/The_Crickets "The Crickets"). The first Tornado Jam was a fundraiser to help Lubbock after [the Tornado](/wiki/Lubbock_tornado "Lubbock tornado"), hence the name. The second Annual Tornado Jam drew a crowd of 35,000.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In the early 1980s, Ely toured with the Kinks, the Rolling Stones and Bruce Springsteen.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the 1990s, he collaborated with Dutch [flamenco](/wiki/Flamenco "Flamenco") guitarist [Teye](/wiki/Teye "Teye"), with whom he recorded *Letter to Laredo* (1995)[[13]](#cite_note-13) and *Twistin' in the Wind* (1998).[[14]](#cite_note-14) Throughout his career Ely issued a steady stream of albums, most on the [MCA](/wiki/Music_Corporation_of_America "Music Corporation of America") label, with a live album every 10 years or so.

In the late 1990s, Ely was asked to write songs for the soundtrack of [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford")'s movie *[The Horse Whisperer](/wiki/The_Horse_Whisperer_(film) "The Horse Whisperer (film)")*, which led to his reforming the Flatlanders with Gilmore and Hancock.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) A new album from the trio followed in 2002, and a third in 2004.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1c/Joe_Ely_2008_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joe_Ely_2008_(cropped).jpg)

Ely in 2008

In February 2007, Ely released *Happy Songs from Rattlesnake Gulch* on his own label, Rack 'Em Records.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) Ely said in an interview with *[Country Standard Time](/wiki/Country_Standard_Time "Country Standard Time")* that he thought it would be easier to release the material on his own label, instead of dealing with a regular record label and their release cycles. A book of Ely's writings, *Bonfire of Roadmaps*, was published in early 2007 by the [University of Texas Press](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Press "University of Texas Press"). In early 2008, Ely released a new live album featuring Joel Guzman on [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion"), recorded at the Cactus Cafe in Austin, Texas, in late 2006.

The Flatlanders released their album *Hills and Valleys* on March 31, 2009.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2011, Ely released the critically acclaimed[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] album, *Satisfied at Last*.[[3]](#cite_note-AMG-3) "Treasure of Love" by the Flatlanders was released in 2021 on Ely's Rack'em Records.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In September 2015, Ely released *Panhandle Rambler,* an album with a reflective [West Texas](/wiki/West_Texas "West Texas") feel. *Lonestar Music* magazine said, "the title fits the record just right, neatly framing a dozen songs that fit together thematically like a map of both the West Texas landscape and of Ely's epic decades-spanning musical ramble."[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Ely spent 2016 as the reigning "Texas State Musician", a one-year designation that he formally accepted in a ceremony at the State Legislature that spring.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In October 2022, he was inducted to the *[Austin City Limits](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits "Austin City Limits")* Hall of Fame.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Illness and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Ely&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Illness and death")]

In September 2025, Ely announced he had been diagnosed with [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease") and [Lewy body dementia](/wiki/Lewy_body_dementia "Lewy body dementia").[[18]](#cite_note-18) He died from Parkinson's, dementia and pneumonia at his [Taos, New Mexico](/wiki/Taos,_New_Mexico "Taos, New Mexico") home, on December 15, 2025, at the age of 78.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Lawsuit against Universal Music Group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Ely&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lawsuit against Universal Music Group")]

On February 5, 2019, Ely and [John Waite](/wiki/John_Waite "John Waite") filed a class-action lawsuit against [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") (UMG) claiming that the company was violating their right to terminate grants of copyright after 35 years, in accordance with [copyright law of the United States](/wiki/Copyright_law_of_the_United_States "Copyright law of the United States"), by ignoring notices of termination. On May 3, 2019, UMG filed a motion to dismiss the case, stating that the notices of termination were not valid because the songs were not grants of copyright, but [works for hire](/wiki/Work_for_hire "Work for hire").[[21]](#cite_note-LeoFre-21)[[22]](#cite_note-DMN-22)

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Ely&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Discography")]
